title: Steps and Learning Rates
concept: The learning rate controls the size of each step in Gradient Descent, balancing speed (too small takes forever) and accuracy (too big overshoots).

exercises.prompt: Recap: In the foggy mountain analogy, what does the bottom of the valley represent?

options.text: The state where model error is minimized
The point of maximum possible loss

exercises.explain: The valley bottom is the minimum of the cost function, representing the lowest possible prediction error.

cards.title: Giant leaps vs baby steps
cards.body: Imagine hiking down a mountain. If you take giant, reckless leaps, you might fly right over the valley. If you crawl on your knees, you will starve before you arrive.

cards.title: Learning rate
cards.body: The learning rate (often called alpha or α) is a configuration parameter that determines the step size at each iteration of Gradient Descent.

cards.title: Adjusting your stride
cards.body: If you are far from the valley, you want to take decent steps. But as you get closer to the cabin, you must shorten your stride to avoid stepping past it.

cards.title: The learning rate dial
cards.body: If we set the learning rate too high, the model's weights bounce back and forth, getting worse. If we set it too low, training takes days instead of minutes.

cards.title: No perfect default
cards.body: There is no single 'best' learning rate for every AI model. Machine learning engineers must experiment to find the right step size for their specific dataset.

cards.title: Learning rate effects
cards.body: How step size settings impact finding the minimum:

left.text:
Stuck on the slope.
Extremely slow training; might get stuck in minor dips.

right.text:
Overshooting the valley.
Bounces back and forth, completely missing the bottom.

exercises.prompt: What happens if the learning rate of a model is set too high?

options.text: The model will converge to the minimum instantly
The steps will overshoot the minimum and the error may increase
The training process will stop automatically

exercises.explain: A high learning rate causes the steps to overshoot the minimum, making the model's error bounce around or diverge.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A very small learning rate is always ideal because it guarantees finding the bottom safely.
exercises.explain: False! While safe, a tiny learning rate makes training painfully slow and makes the model more likely to get stuck before reaching the best valley.

exercises.sentence: The mathematical symbol commonly used to represent the learning rate is ___.
exercises.explain: Alpha (represented by the Greek letter α) is the standard notation for the learning rate parameter.

exercises.prompt: Which learning rate behavior shows that the step size is set correctly?

options.text: The cost function error steadily and smoothly decreases with each iteration
The error keeps jumping up and down, never settling
The error does not change at all after hours of training

exercises.explain: A well-tuned learning rate shows a smooth, steady decline in error until it stabilizes at the minimum.

exercises.prompt: You start training a model and notice the error is actually getting LARGER with each step. What is the most likely problem and fix?

options.text: The learning rate is too high; you should decrease it to take smaller, more stable steps
The learning rate is too low; you should increase it to jump faster
The dataset is too small; you should collect more data to fix the steps

exercises.explain: Fantastic! When the error increases, it means the model is overshooting the valley due to a learning rate that is too high. Reducing it restores stability. Great job on mastering learning rates!
